nativeRange.habitat: The Spotted Hillstream Loach is native to fast-flowing hill streams and torrents with rocky substrates heavily coated in biofilm and aufwuchs — the community of algae, diatoms, and micro-organisms that grow on submerged rocks. The water in these habitats is clear, cool, and low in dissolved nutrients.

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn rocks and pebbles for grazing and clinging
flat slate or broad stones as territory anchors
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
driftwood for additional biofilm surface area
strong lighting to encourage algae and biofilm growth

diet.notes: Obligate aufwuchs grazer in the wild, rasping biofilm, algae, and associated micro-organisms from rocks and hard surfaces. In aquaria, a mature tank with well-established algae and biofilm is essential for long-term health. Supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based foods, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber), and occasionally frozen daphnia or brine shrimp. Avoid protein-heavy diets — excess animal protein causes internal issues in this species. Sinking wafers and gel foods are accepted. Feed multiple small portions throughout the day; this species grazes almost continuously during active hours.

goodWith: small rasboras (e.g., chili rasbora, harlequin rasbora)
hillstream loaches of the same or similar species
bamboo shrimp and vampire shrimp (large filter-feeding shrimp)
avoidWith: fin-nippers or fast competitive feeders that outcompete slow grazers
dwarf frogs (may compete for food and harass loaches)

summary: A small, disc-bodied hillstream loach from fast-flowing Borneo streams, the Spotted Hillstream Loach clings to rocks and grazes biofilm, algae, and the micro-organisms that coat hard surfaces. It requires cool (20–24°C), highly oxygenated, high-flow water and a mature, well-lit tank. Intermediate care due to strict water quality and temperature demands.
careNotes:
The Spotted Hillstream Loach (Gastromyzon punctulatus) is a striking fish with a strongly flattened, disc-like body adapted to life in the high-gradient streams of Borneo. Its pectoral and pelvic fins are modified into a suction apparatus that allows it to cling to smooth rocks in powerful torrents. In the aquarium, replicating this high-flow environment is the most critical care requirement: filtration and powerheads delivering 10–15× tank volume per hour are strongly recommended. Water must be cool (20–24°C) and highly oxygenated — elevated temperatures reduce dissolved oxygen and cause color fading and health decline. A mature, well-lit tank with extensive algae and biofilm growth on rocks is essential; newly set-up tanks are unsuitable as the biofilm layer that sustains this species takes weeks to establish.

Keep in groups of 4 or more. Solitary individuals become stressed and disease-prone; groups display natural aggregation and territory-negotiation behavior. Some individuals may show minor feeding-spot territoriality, but overt aggression is rare. The species can climb glass and smooth surfaces, so a tight-fitting lid is necessary to prevent escape.

Diet is primarily herbivorous — aufwuchs (the community of algae, diatoms, and micro-organisms coating rocks) forms the bulk of their natural intake. In captivity, supplemental feeding with algae wafers, spirulina flakes, blanched zucchini or cucumber, and occasional frozen daphnia is beneficial. Avoid overfeeding protein-rich foods; excess animal protein is associated with internal problems in Gastromyzon species. This species is a continuous grazer and benefits from frequent small feedings.

Water hardness should be soft to moderately soft (gH 2–12, kH 1–8); hard water is poorly tolerated. Keep nitrates below 20 mg/L with consistent partial water changes. The species is sensitive to poor water quality and declining oxygen levels.

Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for G. punctulatus. Related species (G. scitulus) have been spawned under controlled conditions using an 8-week temperature ramp to 28°C followed by a drop to trigger spawning, but this remains experimental.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably achieved for Gastromyzon punctulatus. No established captive breeding protocol exists for this species specifically. Research on related Gastromyzon species suggests temperature manipulation — gradually raising temperature to 28°C over several weeks, then reducing sharply — may stimulate spawning, but results are inconsistent and not reproducible on demand. Eggs are extremely small (under 1 mm) and the hatched fry require microscopic live foods such as infusoria — single-celled organisms cultured specifically for newly hatched fry — in their earliest days. Aquarists interested in breeding should consult specialist hillstream loach forums and ensure the tank is very mature with ample biofilm before attempting.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Hillstream Loaches
primary.notes: Primary trade reference. Covers Gastromyzon and related hillstream loach genera; temperature 20–27°C, flow requirements, diet guidance (algae insufficient alone — supplement with sinking foods), group keeping, tank setup.

additional.name: FishBase — Gastromyzon punctulatus
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 6.5 cm SL, temperature 23–25°C, native range Borneo, family Gastromyzontidae, order Cypriniformes, trophic level 2.1 (herbivore/detritivore). IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Spotted Borneo Sucker (Gastromyzon punctulatus)
additional.notes: Species-specific trade page: adult size 3.5–4 cm, temperature 20–24°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 4–8 dKH, 10–15× flow, minimum 20 gal group tank, biofilm grazer with limited frozen food tolerance, Malaysian Borneo native range.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder — Borneo Sucker Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Gastromyzon spp. care: temperature 18–25°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 2–15 dGH, 10–15× turnover, lifespan 3–4 years. Breeding not achieved in captivity for the genus. Group of 4+ recommended.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Gastromyzon stellatus (congener reference)
additional.notes: Used as congener reference for water parameters: temperature 20–24°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–215 ppm (approx. 1–12 dGH), tank dimensions 75×30 cm minimum, diet aufwuchs + dried spirulina foods, high protein causing internal problems, tight lid required. G. punctulatus page not available on SeriouslyFish.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding documented for G. punctulatus. Temperature-manipulation protocols used for related species (G. scitulus) have not been reproducibly demonstrated for this species.
